content.text: [Image #1] I've got yellow dots in this chart, but there's no activity going on. What is the root cause of this? I can't have this bug in my system. Use 5.6 Sol if you need any help.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codex:codex-rescue: Proactively use when Claude Code is stuck, wants a second implementation or diagnosis pass, needs a deeper root-cause investigation, or should hand a substantial coding task to Codex through the shared runtime (Tools: Bash)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- project-structure-validator: Validates project structure against co-location and architecture patterns defined in AGENTS.md (Tools: All tools)

- sol: General-purpose subagent running on Codex GPT-5.6 Sol via the claude-all gateway. Use for heavy implementation, deep debugging, second-opinion reviews, or any Task the orchestrator wants executed by GPT-5.6 Sol instead of a Claude model. Accepts any prompt a general-purpose agent would. (Tools: All tools)
- spark: General-purpose subagent running on Codex GPT-5.3 Spark (fast) via the claude-all gateway. Use for quick mechanical subtasks, bulk edits, and speed-over-depth work. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

attachment.content:
- access-new-relic-logs: Use when you or the user wants to investigate logs for app.
- access-outlook-business-email: Use when you or the user wants to read, search, triage, or reason about a Microsoft 365 business mailbox, or draft a reply or new email for the user to review and send. This skill never sends — it only creates drafts.
- access-xero-for-px: Full Xero API control for the "Px Internal" Custom Connection (machine-to-machine, single org Pharmacy-Xpress). Use when you or the user wants to read or write Xero data for the PX organisation — invoices, contacts, payments, items, bank transactions, reports, payroll, files, assets. Authenticates via OAuth2 client_credentials and exposes a generic HTTP passthrough to any Xero API endpoint.
- anvil-yaml: Update Anvil app manifest files in `anvil.yaml`, including services, app metadata, and `db_schema`, using the Anvil YAML type reference available to this agent and `anvil --json validate`.
- brand-guidelines: Applies Anthropic's official brand colors and typography to any sort of artifact that may benefit from having Anthropic's look-and-feel. Use it when brand colors or style guidelines, visual formatting, or company design standards apply.
- call-another-llm: Call Codex CLI or Claude Code CLI with a prompt and save the response to tmp/llm-responses/. Use when you need a second opinion, want to offload a subtask, or compare answers across LLMs.
- canvas-design: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. You should use this skill when the user asks to create a poster, piece of art, design, or other static piece. Create original visual designs, never copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- chatgpt-pro-browser-query: Query ChatGPT through the user's already signed-in browser session while proving ChatGPT Pro and Extended Pro are active before sending. Use this whenever the user asks an agent to ask ChatGPT, use ChatGPT Pro, compare with ChatGPT, query the ChatGPT web app, or get a second opinion specifically from their ChatGPT subscription instead of the API.
- chrome-control: Shared Chrome DevTools control bridge that owns the single CDP connection so multiple Chrome-using skills run from one Chrome "Allow control" approval. Use as STEP 1 of any skill that drives the user's Chrome (ChatGPT browser query, ChatGPT image generation, future browser-driving skills).
- chrome-devtools-cli: Use this skill to write shell scripts or run shell commands to automate tasks in the browser or otherwise use Chrome DevTools via CLI.
- claude-epic-pr-review: Token-intensive, maximal review of a large pull request or branch. Runs three parallel review streams — a 5-agent Codex xhigh swarm with overlapping coverage, an adversarial multi-agent epic-review workflow, and a fork that validates every existing PR comment after checks pass — then consolidates all findings, auto-fixes valid code bugs in parallel while asking the user about UX / DB-schema / new-file decisions, runs /code-review max + /simplify max, and replaces the PR. Use for large, high-risk, or cross-cutting changes when an exhaustive, deliberately expensive review is wanted.
- claude-fork-mode: Operate in fork-first mode — delegate all work to background forks (the Agent tool with `subagent_type "fork"`) that inherit the full conversation, so heavy, noisy, or parallel work stays out of the main context window and only compact reports return. Use when the user says "fork mode", "use forks for everything", "delegate everything to forks", or wants to preserve the main agent's context by running work in forks.
- claude-team-review-plan-implementation: Launch a large team of agents (Codex, Opus teammates) to line-by-line compare a reference plan against its implementation across multiple repos, extract deviations, validate, fix, verify, triage design decisions with the user, simplify, and create PRs. Accepts a reference file name as its argument.
- claude-workflow-mode: Operate in workflow-first mode — the main agent becomes an orchestrator that routes single-threaded work to background forks (the Agent tool with `subagent_type "fork"`, which inherit full conversation context and share the prompt cache) and routes ALL parallelizable work to the Workflow tool (deterministic multi-agent fan-out). Single serial stream → one fork; anything that can run concurrently → a workflow. TOKEN-HEAVY — this mode scales comprehensively by default, spawning many subagents per task and burning a large amount of tokens — only use it when that cost is acceptable. Use when the user says "workflow mode", "workflow-first mode", "use workflows for parallel work", or wants parallel work orchestrated through workflows while keeping the main agent's context lean.
- client-forms: Create, inspect, edit, style, validate, and convert Anvil client Forms under `client_code`, including Form Python, HTML templates, event wiring, Data Bindings, RepeatingPanel item templates, reusable Forms, theme CSS, and legacy YAML templates.
- client-modules: Edit Anvil client code Python modules under `client_code` that are not form definitions, without touching read-only dependencies or form templates.
- codex-image-generation: Generate or edit images through Codex CLI's built-in image_gen tool, driven headlessly via `codex exec` on the user's ChatGPT subscription (no OPENAI_API_KEY, no browser). Use whenever the user asks an agent to "generate an image", "make an image with Codex/ChatGPT", edit an existing image, produce icons/mock-ups/hero art/social assets, or create transparent-background PNGs. Supports single generate, multi-prompt batch, image editing, and chroma-key transparency. Final images are written to `<cwd>/tmp/codex-image-generation` by default (override with `--output-dir`).
- data-models: Design idiomatic Anvil data models and client/server data flow for new apps, CRUD workflows, Data Tables, model classes, buffering, drafts, Data Bindings, and server methods. Use before choosing an architecture for persisted domain data, when the user asks about Anvil models/model classes, or when deciding whether to pass rows, model objects, dicts, or server-callable payloads.
- doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- heavy-swarm-plan: Build a read-only implementation plan for complex, multi-area work and pressure-test that plan with Codex CLI and Claude Code CLI before implementation starts. Use when the task is large, ambiguous, cross-cutting, or risky enough that a normal quick plan is not sufficient.
- implement-good-test-patterns: Use datasette-enrichments as a testing reference and imitate its pytest patterns when adding or restructuring tests. Use when writing tests, improving test structure, or aligning a Python project with good integration-style pytest patterns.
- initiate-smoke-tests: Interactive smoke-test runner. Reset the Neon test branch, patch in the fixtures the tests need, then walk the user through each UI smoke test one at a time via AskUser — the question names the exact app, care home, patient(s), med(s), and action. Forks (`subagent_type "fork"`, full context) do every DB sanity check, doc status update, and bug fix. Use when the user says "initiate smoke tests", "let's run the smoke tests", "start/resume smoke testing", or wants to execute a smoke-test doc against the test environment.
- mailshot-carehomes: Use when you or the user wants to send a single bulk announcement (mailshot) to every care-home portal contact - e.g. a new branding link, a portal change, a service notice. Gathers all care-home recipient emails from the Anvil carehome_portal_users table and the Neon "PX" script-copy table, then drafts ONE email (FROM team@, CC stacey@, BCC every care-home contact + zoe@/paresh@/mandeep@) via the access-outlook-business-email skill. Never sends - it only creates a draft for the user to review and send.
- manage-m365-tenant: Use when you or the user wants to administer the Microsoft 365 tenancy — users, groups, licenses, roles, domains, devices, policies, security alerts, audit logs, SharePoint sites, Teams, and usage reports. Full read/write control via Microsoft Graph.
- mk-feature-plan-mode: Plan-only feature design mode. The user states a feature objective; read-only forks map the existing codebase (data model, screens, modules) while the main agent grills the user via AskUser to lock down every UX edge case and scenario. The locked plan is then reviewed by ~2 parallel Codex agents plus an architecture-review sub-agent; any new gaps loop back to the user. Once locked, it writes a feature_plan_<feature>.html design doc to the cwd (UI screens, schema changes, file-tree of new/changed files, out-of-scope) and opens it in the user's default browser, plus a migration.py (test branch by default, --live for prod) only when the schema changes are not purely additive. Use when the user says "feature plan mode", "plan this feature", "design doc for ...", or wants a reviewable UX/architecture plan before any code is written.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- performance-metrics-from-new-relic: Use when you or the user wants frontend performance metrics from New Relic, including Largest Contentful Paint, Interaction to Next Paint, Cumulative Layout Shift, poor distributions, and worst URLs or sessions.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- pr-swarm-review: Fetch comments from an open GitHub pull request, then use a single sub-agent to extract every reviewer's substantive concerns and validate each one against the codebase in one pass, and consolidate the verdicts back in the main agent for triage and fixing. Use when the user wants existing PR comments triaged before deciding what to fix.
- quick-smoke-tests: Generate an ordered, dependency-aware list of UI smoke tests for the work just completed in this session. Walk the user through every test using the AskUser tool — one question per test with all details embedded in the question — then validate each reported result against the database. Use when the user says "smoke test what we just did", "let's quickly verify the changes in the UI", or similar after implementation work.
- server-code: Edit Anvil server code Python modules under `server_code`, including server-callable functions, background logic, and server packages, while keeping changes isolated to the app workspace.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- sync-global-user-instructions-md: Sync one canonical global instruction file into user-global Codex and Claude instruction files. Use when you need to update the canonical user-global instructions, diff installed global files against canon, reconcile drift, or replace user-global AGENTS/CLAUDE files from the canonical source.
- sync-mcps: Sync canonical MCP server manifests into Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Cursor config files. Use when you need to add, remove, or update an MCP across all agents from one source of truth, or to diff installed MCP configs against canon.
- theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs.
- windows-computer-use: Drive the Windows desktop UI the way a user can — list monitors/windows, read UIA element trees, click/right-click/double-click, type text, send key chords, scroll, drag, and capture screenshots across multiple monitors and any app type. Use when a task needs native Windows app or OS control beyond the browser (File Explorer, Settings, installers, system dialogs, Office desktop, WinUI/UWP/Win32 apps). For controlling a web browser, prefer chrome-control / the browser skills instead.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- astral:ruff: Guide for using ruff, the extremely fast Python linter and formatter. Use this when linting, formatting, or fixing Python code.
- astral:ty: Guide for using ty, the extremely fast Python type checker and language server. Use this when type checking Python code or setting up type checking in Python projects.
- astral:uv: Guide for using uv, the Python package and project manager. Use this when working with Python projects, scripts, packages, or tools.
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- understand-anything:understand: Analyze a codebase to produce an interactive knowledge graph for understanding architecture, components, and relationships
- understand-anything:understand-chat: Use when you need to ask questions about a codebase or understand code using a knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-dashboard: Launch the interactive web dashboard to visualize a codebase's knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-diff: Use when you need to analyze git diffs or pull requests to understand what changed, affected components, and risks
- understand-anything:understand-domain: Extract business domain knowledge from a codebase and generate an interactive domain flow graph. Works standalone (lightweight scan) or derives from an existing /understand knowledge graph.
- understand-anything:understand-explain: Use when you need a deep-dive explanation of a specific file, function, or module in the codebase
- understand-anything:understand-knowledge: Analyze a Karpathy-pattern LLM wiki knowledge base and generate an interactive knowledge graph with entity extraction, implicit relationships, and topic clustering.
- understand-anything:understand-onboard: Use when you need to generate an onboarding guide for new team members joining a project
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: [Image #1] I've got yellow dots in this chart, but there's no activity going on. What is the root cause of this? I can't have this bug in my system. Use 5.6 Sol if you need any help.

aiTitle: Debug spurious yellow dots in chart

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codex:codex-rescue: Proactively use when Claude Code is stuck, wants a second implementation or diagnosis pass, needs a deeper root-cause investigation, or should hand a substantial coding task to Codex through the shared runtime (Tools: Bash)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- project-structure-validator: Validates project structure against co-location and architecture patterns defined in AGENTS.md (Tools: All tools)

- sol: General-purpose subagent running on Codex GPT-5.6 Sol via the claude-all gateway. Use for heavy implementation, deep debugging, second-opinion reviews, or any Task the orchestrator wants executed by GPT-5.6 Sol instead of a Claude model. Accepts any prompt a general-purpose agent would. (Tools: All tools)
- spark: General-purpose subagent running on Codex GPT-5.3 Spark (fast) via the claude-all gateway. Use for quick mechanical subtasks, bulk edits, and speed-over-depth work. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

content: [Image #1] I've got yellow dots in this chat, but there's no activity going on. What is the root cause of this? I can't have this bug in my system. Use 5.6 Sol if you need any help

questions.question: Message "this chat not chart" lacks context — no prior turn in this session. What do you mean?

options.label: Correcting a typo
options.description: You meant "chat" where something said "chart" — tell me where (file, message, feature)

options.label: Do something with this chat
options.description: Summarize / export / continue work in this conversation — specify what

options.label: Wrong window
options.description: Message meant for another session — ignore it

# (MARKER-RECONCILE) only a payload that actually carries the list shape
    # is authoritative; absent/odd-shaped -> None -> no reconciliation (an
    # older Claude Code without background_tasks behaves exactly as before).
    bg_ids = _running_bg_ids(bg_tasks) if isinstance(bg_tasks, (list, dict)) else None
    if team_note:
        _log({
            "event": event,
            "terminalId": terminal_id,
            "sessionId": session_id,
            "action": "teammate-idle-drop",
            "note": team_note[0],
        })

    # (BA diagnostic) When background_tasks is non-empty, dump its shape so we can
    # tell an actively-working teammate/subagent (should be YELLOW) apart from a
    # passive background shell/cloud session (BLUE). Truncated; never raises.
    if has_background:
        try:
            _log({
                "event": event,
                "terminalId": terminal_id,
                "sessionId": session_id,
                "action": "bg-tasks-debug",
                "bgCount": len(bg_tasks) if isinstance(bg_tasks, list) else -1,
                "bgTasks": json.dumps(bg_tasks)[:1500],
            })
        except Exception:
            pass

    if not terminal_id:
        _log({
            "event": event, "tool": tool, "mappedEventType": None,
            "terminalId": terminal_id, "sessionId": session_id, "url": url,
            "action": "skip-no-terminal",
        })
        return

    # (PANE-MAP-UNSTEAL) the ending session's pane mapping must not outlive it
    # on this terminal (see _drop_pane_map_if_ours).
    if event == "SessionEnd":
        _drop_pane_map_if_ours(session_id, terminal_id)

    # Also performs the SubagentStart/Stop marker side-effects, so the
    # yellow-hold state stays consistent even when url is momentarily absent.
    reason_out = []
    event_type = _decide_event_type(
        event,
        tool,
        terminal_id,
        sub_agent_id,
        has_background,

options.label: Per-entry id→name match (Recommended)
options.description: Match each running teammate bg-entry to its ledger name via prompt-prefix (payload description ↔ Agent spawn prompt). Drop only entries whose name is provably idle. Immune to ledger poison, no false-green window. More complex — will implement + Sol/Fable review, ship via full build.

options.label: Simple predicate filter
options.description: All-idle predicate only counts names that ever sent a teammate-message (proven teammates); also fix SendMessage-fork re-add. Small + safe-ish, BUT a freshly spawned teammate that hasn't messaged yet could be dropped → brief false-green window.

content.content:
900	        if task_type == "shell":
901	            has_shell = True
902	        else:
903	            has_agent = True
904	    return (has_any, has_agent, has_shell)
905	
906	
907	# (TEAMMATE-IDLE) Claude Code NEVER flips an idle teammate's background_tasks[]
908	# entry off "running" (captured live 2026-07-10: 33 teammate entries, all
909	# status "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had ALL finished hours
910	# earlier), so the payload alone cannot green a lead whose teammates are done —
911	# once no further hook event arrived, the dot stayed yellow forever. The lead
912	# TRANSCRIPT has the missing truth: every teammate that finishes delivers an
913	# idle_notification teammate-message into it, and every (re)activation is
914	# visible too (an Agent spawn tool_use, a SendMessage tool_use to it, a
915	# non-idle teammate-message/agent-message from it). Named fork Agent spawns are
916	# NOT teammates: their subagent_type is "fork", and they never emit teammate
917	# idle_notification messages. Exclude them at spawn and map their tool-use-id
918	# to the name so a deterministic completed task-notification can remove any
919	# legacy/reintroduced entry. Track the teammate ledger incrementally and, at a
920	# turn end, DROP unfinished teammate-type entries when every tracked teammate's
921	# last event is an idle_notification. POSITIVE proof only: no transcript, no
922	# teammates seen, a parse error, an unmatched completion, or ANY teammate not
923	# provably idle -> keep the entries (the safe yellow direction — exactly the
924	# old behaviour, with the (BG-STALE) idle reap still behind it). A teammate
925	# that dies WITHOUT ever reporting stays "active" in the ledger and keeps the
926	# hold: from the logs that case is indistinguishable from a long think, so it
927	# is deliberately left to (BG-STALE).
928	
929	
930	def _team_scan_text(state, text):
931	    # Update the name -> "active"|"idle" ledger from one transcript text blob.
932	    # String scanning only (NO regex): this file is a TS template literal, and
933	    # single backslashes in regex escapes would be silently swallowed.
934	    i = 0
935	    tag = '<teammate-message teammate_id="'
936	    while True:
937	        i = text.find(tag, i)
938	        if i < 0:
939	            break
940	        j = i + len(tag)
941	        k = text.find('"', j)
942	        if k < 0:
943	            break
944	        name = text[j:k]
945	        end = text.find("</teammate-message>", k)
946	        body = text[k:end] if end > 0 else text[k:]
947	        if name:
948	            state[name] = "idle" if '"idle_notification"' in body else "active"
949	        i = k
950	    a = 0
951	    tag2 = '<agent-message from="'
952	    while True:
953	        a = text.find(tag2, a)
954	        if a < 0:
955	            break
956	        b = a + len(tag2)
957	        c = text.find('"', b)
958	        if c < 0:
959	            break
960	        if text[b:c]:
961	            state[text[b:c]] = "active"
962	        a = c
963	
964	
965	def _team_scan_completion(state, fork_tools, text):
966	    # Fork completion notices deterministically carry the spawning Agent
967	    # tool-use-id. Only a completed notice with an exact mapped id may remove a
968	    # name; malformed/unmatched text leaves the ledger untouched (safe yellow).
969	    if not text.startswith("<task-notification>"):
970	        return
971	    if "<status>completed</status>" not in text:
972	        return
973	    tag = "<tool-use-id>"
974	    i = text.find(tag)
975	    if i < 0:
976	        return
977	    j = i + len(tag)
978	    k = text.find("</tool-use-id>", j)
979	    if k < 0:
980	        return
981	    tool_use_id = text[j:k].strip()
982	    name = fork_tools.get(tool_use_id)
983	    if name:
984	        state.pop(name, None)
985	
986	
987	def _team_scan_record(state, fork_tools, obj):
988	    # One JSONL transcript record -> ledger updates. Spawning a non-fork Agent
989	    # with a name or waking it via SendMessage marks it active. Named forks are
990	    # excluded and indexed by Agent tool-use-id so a completed task-notification
991	    # can untrack stale state. Teammate/agent message text decides idle vs active.
992	    top_content = obj.get("content")
993	    if isinstance(top_content, str):
994	        _team_scan_completion(state, fork_tools, top_content)
995	    msg = obj.get("message") or {}
996	    if not isinstance(msg, dict):
997	        return
998	    content = msg.get("content")
999	    if isinstance(content, str):
1000	        _team_scan_text(state, content)
1001	        _team_scan_completion(state, fork_tools, content)
1002	        return
1003	    if not isinstance(content, list):
1004	        return
1005	    for c in content:
1006	        if not isinstance(c, dict):
1007	            continue
1008	        ctype = c.get("type")
1009	        if ctype == "text":
1010	            text = str(c.get("text") or "")
1011	            _team_scan_text(state, text)
1012	            _team_scan_completion(state, fork_tools, text)
1013	        elif ctype == "tool_use":
1014	            inp = c.get("input")
1015	            if not isinstance(inp, dict):
1016	                continue
1017	            if c.get("name") == "Agent":
1018	                n = str(inp.get("name") or "").strip()
1019	                if n and str(inp.get("subagent_type") or "").strip() == "fork":
1020	                    tool_use_id = str(c.get("id") or "").strip()
1021	                    if tool_use_id:
1022	                        fork_tools[tool_use_id] = n
1023	                    state.pop(n, None)
1024	                elif n:
1025	                    state[n] = "active"
1026	            elif c.get("name") == "SendMessage":
1027	                n = str(inp.get("to") or "").strip()
1028	                if n:
1029	                    state[n] = "active"
1030	
1031	
1032	def _team_is_fork_id(name):
1033	    # A raw agentId (observed shape: "a" + >=12 hex chars) is a FORK handle,
1034	    # not a teammate name. Forks fire real SubagentStart/Stop hooks and their
1035	    # bg entries are type "subagent" — both already handled — and they never
1036	    # send idle_notification, so counting one here would pin the ledger
1037	    # un-idle forever. Excluded from the all-idle predicate.
1038	    if len(name) < 13 or name[0] != "a":
1039	        return False
1040	    return all(ch in "0123456789abcdef" for ch in name[1:])
1041	
1042	
1043	def _teammates_all_idle(transcript_path, terminal_id):
1044	    # True only on POSITIVE transcript proof that every tracked teammate's
1045	    # last event is an idle_notification. Incremental: consumes only bytes
1046	    # appended since the cached offset (the first call pays one full read;
1047	    # transcripts are append-only, surviving /compact). Never raises; every
1048	    # failure path returns False (keep the hold).
1049	    if not transcript_path or not terminal_id:
1050	        return False
1051	    cache_file = _teamstate_path(terminal_id)
1052	    state = {}
1053	    fork_tools = {}
1054	    offset = 0
1055	    try:
1056	        rec = json.loads(cache_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
1057	        if (
1058	            isinstance(rec, dict)
1059	            and rec.get("version") == 2
1060	            and rec.get("path") == transcript_path
1061	            and isinstance(rec.get("state"), dict)
1062	            and isinstance(rec.get("forkTools"), dict)
1063	        ):
1064	            offset = int(rec.get("offset") or 0)
1065	            for k, v in rec["state"].items():
1066	                state[str(k)] = str(v)
1067	            for k, v in rec["forkTools"].items():
1068	                fork_tools[str(k)] = str(v)
1069	    except Exception:
1070	        state = {}
1071	        fork_tools = {}
1072	        offset = 0
1073	    try:
1074	        with open(transcript_path, "rb") as h:
1075	            h.seek(0, 2)
1076	            size = h.tell()
1077	            if offset < 0 or offset > size:
1078	                offset = 0
1079	                state = {}
1080	                fork_tools = {}
1081	            h.seek(offset)
1082	            data = h.read()
1083	    except Exception:
1084	        return False
1085	    nl = data.rfind(b"\\n")
1086	    if nl >= 0:
1087	        for raw in data[:nl].split(b"\\n"):
1088	            if not raw.strip():
1089	                continue
1090	            try:
1091	                obj = json.loads(raw.decode("utf-8", "replace"))
1092	            except Exception:
1093	                continue
1094	            if isinstance(obj, dict):
1095	                _team_scan_record(state, fork_tools, obj)
1096	        offset = offset + nl + 1
1097	    try:
1098	        cache_file.write_text(
1099	            json.dumps({
1100	                "version": 2,
1101	                "path": transcript_path,
1102	                "offset": offset,
1103	                "state": state,
1104	                "forkTools": fork_tools,
1105	            }),
1106	            encoding="utf-8",
1107	        )
1108	    except Exception:
1109	        pass
1110	    tracked = {}
1111	    for name, st in state.items():
1112	        if name in ("team-lead", "main") or _team_is_fork_id(name):
1113	            continue
1114	        tracked[name] = st
1115	    if not tracked:
1116	        return False
1117	    for st in tracked.values():
1118	        if st != "idle":
1119	            return False
1120	    return True
1121	
1122	
1123	def _without_idle_teammates(bg_tasks, transcript_path, terminal_id, note_out=None):
1124	    # (TEAMMATE-IDLE) Returns bg_tasks with unfinished teammate-type entries
1125	    # dropped when the transcript ledger proves all teammates idle; otherwise
1126	    # returns bg_tasks unchanged. Only ever REMOVES teammate entries — shell /
1127	    # subagent / workflow entries always pass through untouched. Never raises.
1128	    if not isinstance(bg_tasks, list) or not bg_tasks:
1129	        return bg_tasks
1130	    has_teammate = False
1131	    for t in bg_tasks:
1132	        if (
1133	            isinstance(t, dict)
1134	            and not _bg_entry_finished(t)
1135	            and str(t.get("type") or "") == "teammate"
1136	        ):
1137	            has_teammate = True

url = os.environ.get("SUPERSET_HOST_AGENT_HOOK_URL", "").strip()
    terminal_id = os.environ.get("SUPERSET_TERMINAL_ID", "").strip()
    agent_id = (os.environ.get("SUPERSET_AGENT_ID") or "claude").strip()

    # (TEAMMATE-IDLE) At the turn-end decision points, drop teammate-type
    # entries the transcript proves idle BEFORE any consumer (the split, the
    # agentbg/shellbg/bgactive snapshot, the hold decision) — the harness
    # reports finished teammates as status "running" forever, so without this
    # a lead whose teammates are all done held yellow until the next event
    # (which, on an idle window, never came). bg_ids stays derived from the
    # RAW list: it is a KEEP-set for marker reconciliation, and teammates
    # never create run-dir markers, so extra ids are harmless while missing
    # ids could reap a genuine fork marker.
    transcript_path = str(
        payload.get("transcript_path") or payload.get("transcriptPath") or ""
    ).strip()
    team_note = []
    bg_tasks_eff = bg_tasks
    if event in ("Stop", "SubagentStop"):
        bg_tasks_eff = _without_idle_teammates(
            bg_tasks, transcript_path, terminal_id, team_note
        )
    has_background, has_agent_background, has_shell_background = _split_background(
        bg_tasks_eff
    )
    # (MARKER-RECONCILE) only a payload that actually carries the list shape
    # is authoritative; absent/odd-shaped -> None -> no reconciliation (an
    # older Claude Code without background_tasks behaves exactly as before).
    bg_ids = _running_bg_ids(bg_tasks) if isinstance(bg_tasks, (list, dict)) else None
    if team_note:
        _log({
            "event": event,
            "terminalId": terminal_id,
            "sessionId": session_id,
            "action": "teammate-idle-drop",
            "note": team_note[0],
        })

    # (BA diagnostic) When background_tasks is non-empty, dump its shape so we can
    # tell an actively-working teammate/subagent (should be YELLOW) apart from a

content.content:
67:| Idle teammates green deterministically | Claude Code reports finished teammates in the Stop payload's background_tasks[] as `status:"running"` FOREVER (captured live: 33 teammate entries, all "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had all finished hours earlier) — and the (BG-STALE) reap only runs inside hook events, so a session whose LAST event landed while the set was <15 min idle latched yellow indefinitely (nothing ever re-evaluated; 9 terminals observed stuck). superset-notify.py now keeps a per-terminal teammate LEDGER parsed incrementally from the lead session transcript (payload `transcript_path`; offset+state cached in `<terminalId>.teamstate.json`, keyed by transcript path so /resume self-invalidates): Agent-spawn / SendMessage-wake / non-idle teammate- or agent-messages mark a name active, an `idle_notification` teammate-message marks it idle. At Stop/SubagentStop, when EVERY tracked teammate is provably idle, unfinished teammate-type entries are dropped BEFORE the split/snapshot/hold decision — the lead greens (or blues on a shell remainder) at its actual final turn end instead of holding forever. Positive proof only: no transcript / no teammates seen / any parse error / any teammate not idle → entries kept (old behaviour + (BG-STALE) unchanged behind it); raw fork agentIds ("a"+hex) are excluded from the predicate (forks have real hook lifecycles and would never idle-notify) — and NAMED forks are excluded too: the spawn parser reads the Agent tool-use input's `subagent_type`, skips tracking fork spawns regardless of name, and indexes them by tool-use-id so their completed task-notification untracks exactly that spawn (a named fork previously latched "active" forever and made the all-idle predicate unsatisfiable — live 2026-07-21); cache schema v2 forces one full rescan to self-heal already-poisoned ledgers; subagent/workflow/shell entries always pass through untouched; bg_ids marker reconciliation still uses the RAW list. Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` |
140:(TEAMMATE-IDLE)	apps/desktop/src/main

content.text: Writing fix. Replacing global all-idle predicate with per-entry id→name prompt matching.

input.old_string:
# (TEAMMATE-IDLE) Claude Code NEVER flips an idle teammate's background_tasks[]
# entry off "running" (captured live 2026-07-10: 33 teammate entries, all
# status "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had ALL finished hours
# earlier), so the payload alone cannot green a lead whose teammates are done —
# once no further hook event arrived, the dot stayed yellow forever. The lead
# TRANSCRIPT has the missing truth: every teammate that finishes delivers an
# idle_notification teammate-message into it, and every (re)activation is
# visible too (an Agent spawn tool_use, a SendMessage tool_use to it, a
# non-idle teammate-message/agent-message from it). Named fork Agent spawns are
# NOT teammates: their subagent_type is "fork", and they never emit teammate
# idle_notification messages. Exclude them at spawn and map their tool-use-id
# to the name so a deterministic completed task-notification can remove any
# legacy/reintroduced entry. Track the teammate ledger incrementally and, at a
# turn end, DROP unfinished teammate-type entries when every tracked teammate's
# last event is an idle_notification. POSITIVE proof only: no transcript, no
# teammates seen, a parse error, an unmatched completion, or ANY teammate not
# provably idle -> keep the entries (the safe yellow direction — exactly the
# old behaviour, with the (BG-STALE) idle reap still behind it). A teammate
# that dies WITHOUT ever reporting stays "active" in the ledger and keeps the
# hold: from the logs that case is indistinguishable from a long think, so it
# is deliberately left to (BG-STALE).
input.new_string:
# (TEAMMATE-IDLE) Claude Code NEVER flips an idle teammate's background_tasks[]
# entry off "running" (captured live 2026-07-10: 33 teammate entries, all
# status "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had ALL finished hours
# earlier), so the payload alone cannot green a lead whose teammates are done —
# once no further hook event arrived, the dot stayed yellow forever. The lead
# TRANSCRIPT has the missing truth: every teammate that finishes delivers an
# idle_notification teammate-message into it, and every (re)activation is
# visible too (an Agent spawn tool_use, a SendMessage tool_use to it, a
# non-idle teammate-message/agent-message from it).
#
# (TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH) The original all-idle predicate (drop teammate entries
# only when EVERY tracked name is idle) proved unsatisfiable in real sessions
# (captured live 2026-07-22: 55 permanently-"active" names — named non-fork
# Agent spawns like sol/general-purpose review agents that finish via
# task-notification and never idle-notify, forks re-marked active by a later
# SendMessage, and "name [hash]" duplicate-suffix splits — held 25 finished
# wrangler teammates yellow all day). The decision is now PER ENTRY: a
# running teammate entry's payload `description` is the spawn prompt's head
# (observed live: first 50 chars + "..."), so match it against the prompt
# prefix recorded at each named Agent spawn; an entry may be dropped only
# when it matches at least one recorded spawn AND every matching name's last
# ledger event is an idle_notification. Unmatched, ambiguous-with-active, or
# unparseable -> keep that entry (the safe yellow direction). Poisoned
# "active" names can no longer block OTHER teammates' drops. A teammate that
# dies WITHOUT ever reporting stays "active" and keeps its own hold: from
# the logs that case is indistinguishable from a long think, so it is
# deliberately left to (BG-STALE).


def _team_norm(text):
    # Collapse whitespace runs to single spaces so a spawn prompt and the
    # payload description derived from it compare equal even if truncation
    # or the harness normalized newlines. String ops only (NO regex: this
    # file is a TS template literal and single backslashes in regex escapes
    # would be silently swallowed).
    return " ".join(str(text or "").split())

import importlib.util, json, time, pathlib
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("notify", "tmp/notify-extracted.py")
m = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(m)

transcript = r"C:\Users\khair\.claude\projects\C--Users-khair--superset-worktrees-59ba6afd-1544-4dbd-9bd5-7dcbbcb4d2e8-popular-throat\b32f0f38-3bf8-4dfb-ae3a-8dc40f7cb0f1.jsonl"
fake_term = "test-team-entry-match"
t0 = time.time()
ledger = m._team_ledger(transcript, fake_term)
t1 = time.time()
assert ledger is not None
state, prompts = ledger
print("parse %.1fs  state=%d prompts=%d" % (t1-t0, len(state), len(prompts)))

# real captured descriptions from bg-tasks-debug (payload truncation included)
real_descs = [
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane SRV1 of the pla...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane SRV2 of the pla...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane SRV3 of the pla...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane CL1 of the plan...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane CL2 of the plan...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane CL3 of the plan...",
  "You are the Opus wrangler for lane DEP of the plan...",
  "You are the FABLE half of the fresh review pair fo...",
  "You are the SOL half of the independent verificati...",
]
entries = [{"id": "t%d" % i, "type": "teammate", "status": "running", "description": d} for i, d in enumerate(real_descs)]
for e in entries:
    names = [n for n, p in prompts.items() if p and p.startswith(m._team_norm(e["description"][:-3]))]
    print("%-55s -> match=%s droppable=%s states=%s" % (e["description"][:52], names[:4], m._team_entry_droppable(e, state, prompts), [state.get(n) for n in names[:4]]))

out = m._without_idle_teammates(entries, transcript, fake_term, note := [])
print("in=%d out=%d note=%s" % (len(entries), len(out), note))
pathlib.Path.home().joinpath(".superset/agent-subagent-running/%s.teamstate.json" % fake_term).unlink(missing_ok=True)
EOF

import importlib.util, pathlib
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("notify", "tmp/notify-extracted.py")

fake = "test-team-entry-match"

  {"id":"x2","type":"teammate","status":"running","description":"totally unknown prompt text that matches nothing..."},  # unmatched -> keep

  {"id":"x4","type":"shell","status":"running","description":"whatever"},                          # non-teammate -> untouched
  {"id":"x5","type":"teammate","status":"completed","description":"done"},                         # finished -> untouched
]
out = m._without_idle_teammates(entries, transcript, fake, note := [])

pathlib.Path.home().joinpath(".superset/agent-subagent-running/%s.teamstate.json" % fake).unlink(missing_ok=True)

input.old_string: Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` |
input.new_string: Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability. (TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH, 2026-07-22): the all-idle predicate proved unsatisfiable in real multi-agent sessions — 55 permanently-"active" ledger names (named non-fork Agent spawns like sol/general-purpose/review agents that finish via task-notification and never idle-notify, forks re-marked active by a later SendMessage, and "name [hash]" duplicate-suffix splits) held 25 finished wrangler teammates yellow all day. The decision is now PER ENTRY: each running teammate entry's `description` (spawn-prompt head, first ~50 chars + "...") is matched against normalized prompt prefixes recorded at every named Agent spawn (cache schema v3 adds `prompts`; v2 caches force one self-healing full rescan); an entry drops only when it matches ≥1 recorded spawn AND every matching name is provably idle — unmatched/ambiguous-with-active/too-short descriptions keep their entry. SendMessage to a known fork name no longer marks it active, and " [hash]"-suffixed teammate-message ids mirror onto the base name so the base tracks the latest same-named instance | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` `(TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH)` |

SendMessage to a known fork name no longer marks it active, and " [hash]"-suffixed teammate-message ids mirror onto the base name so the base tracks the latest same-named instance | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` `(TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH)` |

lines: | Auto-resume stuck chats | when a Claude chat dies on an API failure the JSONL watcher classifies it (deny-list-first: rate-limit-with-reset-time / transient-rate-limit / server-error / connection-drop / half-stop are resumable; auth/policy/bad-image/model-unavailable are notify-only) after a finality/quiescence gate, and a durable MAIN-side registry auto-sends "resume from exactly where everything was left" — fire-at-parsed-reset-time (+30s, DST-correct, fire-now-if-already-past, ≤8d sane cap) for usage limits, else a 60s→×3 backoff capped at 5 sends / 24h. Every send is gated at fire time (toggle on, still the last meaningful transcript line, host-service `commandRunning`=idle + agent-session binding match) so it can never auto-confirm a menu, land mid-turn, or run at a bare shell. Any trusted interaction with that terminal cancels (persisted tombstone survives reload/restart). Account-wide cohorts are jittered + concurrency-capped. Global toggle (default on). Claude-only in v1 — Codex auto-resume is deferred (the on-disk corpus has no validated standalone usage-limit signal). Dots untouched (separate IPC variant + toasts) | `(AUTO-RESUME)`, `autoResumeManager`, `writeInputIfIdleSession`, `useAutoResumeActivity`, `AutoResumeController` |

| Dot-decision log + codex pid-reuse cap | superset-notify.py writes an always-on bounded `~/.superset/logs/dot-decisions.log` line at each terminal turn-end decision (the POSTed eventType + WHY a hold won — codex job / background_tasks / subagent markers / GREEN / BLUE / RED), and `_codex_job_active` age-caps pid-bearing `running` codex job records at 6h so a recycled PID can't pin the dot yellow forever (a cap-skipped stale record is logged, so a premature-green is auditable) | `_decision_log` |

| Idle/zombie teammate hold reaped | an agent set Claude Code still reports `running` in background_tasks[] but that has produced NO activity (SubagentStart / subagent-scoped PostToolUse refresh a `.bgactive` timer) for 15 min stops pinning the lead's dot yellow — the harness never flips an idle long-lived teammate (or one that died without a clean SubagentStop) to a finished status, so the hold previously lasted until the team was disbanded / the session ended. The JSONL watcher re-asserts yellow if a reaped set is in fact still writing. A stale reap of a MIXED set no longer greens live work: `_split_background` tracks live `type:"shell"` entries separately, so zombie agents + running background shells resolve to the blue shell remainder (live 2026-07-21 incident: two running crash/build monitors showed no dot after the zombie-teammate hold staled out); an agent-only zombie set still greens | `(BG-STALE)` |

-| Idle teammates green deterministically | Claude Code reports finished teammates in the Stop payload's background_tasks[] as `status:"running"` FOREVER (captured live: 33 teammate entries, all "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had all finished hours earlier) — and the (BG-STALE) reap only runs inside hook events, so a session whose LAST event landed while the set was <15 min idle latched yellow indefinitely (nothing ever re-evaluated; 9 terminals observed stuck). superset-notify.py now keeps a per-terminal teammate LEDGER parsed incrementally from the lead session transcript (payload `transcript_path`; offset+state cached in `<terminalId>.teamstate.json`, keyed by transcript path so /resume self-invalidates): Agent-spawn / SendMessage-wake / non-idle teammate- or agent-messages mark a name active, an `idle_notification` teammate-message marks it idle. At Stop/SubagentStop, when EVERY tracked teammate is provably idle, unfinished teammate-type entries are dropped BEFORE the split/snapshot/hold decision — the lead greens (or blues on a shell remainder) at its actual final turn end instead of holding forever. Positive proof only: no transcript / no teammates seen / any parse error / any teammate not idle → entries kept (old behaviour + (BG-STALE) unchanged behind it); raw fork agentIds ("a"+hex) are excluded from the predicate (forks have real hook lifecycles and would never idle-notify) — and NAMED forks are excluded too: the spawn parser reads the Agent tool-use input's `subagent_type`, skips tracking fork spawns regardless of name, and indexes them by tool-use-id so their completed task-notification untracks exactly that spawn (a named fork previously latched "active" forever and made the all-idle predicate unsatisfiable — live 2026-07-21); cache schema v2 forces one full rescan to self-heal already-poisoned ledgers; subagent/workflow/shell entries always pass through untouched; bg_ids marker reconciliation still uses the RAW list. Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` |

+| Idle teammates green deterministically | Claude Code reports finished teammates in the Stop payload's background_tasks[] as `status:"running"` FOREVER (captured live: 33 teammate entries, all "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had all finished hours earlier) — and the (BG-STALE) reap only runs inside hook events, so a session whose LAST event landed while the set was <15 min idle latched yellow indefinitely (nothing ever re-evaluated; 9 terminals observed stuck). superset-notify.py now keeps a per-terminal teammate LEDGER parsed incrementally from the lead session transcript (payload `transcript_path`; offset+state cached in `<terminalId>.teamstate.json`, keyed by transcript path so /resume self-invalidates): Agent-spawn / SendMessage-wake / non-idle teammate- or agent-messages mark a name active, an `idle_notification` teammate-message marks it idle. At Stop/SubagentStop, when EVERY tracked teammate is provably idle, unfinished teammate-type entries are dropped BEFORE the split/snapshot/hold decision — the lead greens (or blues on a shell remainder) at its actual final turn end instead of holding forever. Positive proof only: no transcript / no teammates seen / any parse error / any teammate not idle → entries kept (old behaviour + (BG-STALE) unchanged behind it); raw fork agentIds ("a"+hex) are excluded from the predicate (forks have real hook lifecycles and would never idle-notify) — and NAMED forks are excluded too: the spawn parser reads the Agent tool-use input's `subagent_type`, skips tracking fork spawns regardless of name, and indexes them by tool-use-id so their completed task-notification untracks exactly that spawn (a named fork previously latched "active" forever and made the all-idle predicate unsatisfiable — live 2026-07-21); cache schema v2 forces one full rescan to self-heal already-poisoned ledgers; subagent/workflow/shell entries always pass through untouched; bg_ids marker reconciliation still uses the RAW list. Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability. (TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH, 2026-07-22): the all-idle predicate proved unsatisfiable in real multi-agent sessions — 55 permanently-"active" ledger names (named non-fork Agent spawns like sol/general-purpose/review agents that finish via task-notification and never idle-notify, forks re-marked active by a later SendMessage, and "name [hash]" duplicate-suffix splits) held 25 finished wrangler teammates yellow all day. The decision is now PER ENTRY: each running teammate entry's `description` (spawn-prompt head, first ~50 chars + "...") is matched against normalized prompt prefixes recorded at every named Agent spawn (cache schema v3 adds `prompts`; v2 caches force one self-healing full rescan); an entry drops only when it matches ≥1 recorded spawn AND every matching name is provably idle — unmatched/ambiguous-with-active/too-short descriptions keep their entry. SendMessage to a known fork name no longer marks it active, and " [hash]"-suffixed teammate-message ids mirror onto the base name so the base tracks the latest same-named instance | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` `(TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH)` |

| ReferenceError-class build gate | the build (and therefore every nightly, whose build job calls the same workflow) fails on cannot-find-name tsc diagnostics ONLY (TS2304/2552/2662/2663/18004) — bare identifiers esbuild bundles fine but that throw ReferenceError at runtime, exactly what a CLEAN-merging upstream hunk can leave in fork code the AI resolver never sees (v1.14.0 layout.tsx incident: upstream renamed the workspace source variable in a non-conflicting hunk; fork's cache-first hold kept referencing it; every v2 workspace view crashed). Per-package direct tsc (turbo graph is debt-blocked); the accepted type debt has zero of these codes so the gate can't false-abort | `(REFERR-GATE)` |

| Nightly dependency-consistency + lock regen | the nightly merge can introduce dependency-state drift that only the build discovers (after a windows-arm64 runner spins up): a stale fork root `overrides` entry that pins a package to a version different from the merged workspace pin (electron-builder "production dependency not found …"), or a `bun.lock` left stale by an upstream package.json bump (frozen-install fails). The merge now runs an override-consistency gate (fails loud on any exact override↔pin mismatch) + regenerates `bun.lock` to match the merged manifests with the build's exact bun version (which also fails loud, in the cheap merge job, if the merged manifests don't resolve or a pin is age-blocked) — so this class of build failure is caught/fixed before the windows-arm64 build spins up, baseline untouched on failure (recovery loop). No full `--frozen-lockfile` install runs here: the merge runner is linux, so a platform-specific install quirk would be a false abort, and the regenerated lock is consistent with the manifests by construction. The fork carries NO mastra `overrides` and pins `@mastra/core`/`mastracode` to upstream's exact versions in all three workspaces (upstream keeps mastra consistent via identical exact pins; the seed commit's redundant override + a recovery merge that resolved the pins DOWN to 0.18.0 were the recurring breakage, both corrected) | `(LOCK-REGEN)` |

| Fork-owned workflow dir | `.github/workflows/` carries ONLY the fork's own workflows — upstream CI files were dropped at fork creation (they target upstream's infra/secrets) and the Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` can NEVER push a workflow-file change anyway (no `workflows` scope exists for it: "refusing to allow a GitHub App to create or update workflow"). Upstream desktop-v1.14.3 added `deploy-relay.yml`, which merged CLEANLY and then killed the candidate push after every gate had already passed. The nightly now deterministically restores the fork's exact workflow set mid-merge (`git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows`) BEFORE conflict detection, so upstream workflow additions/edits never enter the fork, never reach the AI resolver, and never hit the push restriction — and the dropped upstream CI delta is SURFACED, not silently erased (job summary + log + handed to the semantic review, which appends advisory `CI-NOTE:` lines when upstream moves build-relevant logic into its CI), with a pre-commit postcondition proving the committed tree carries zero workflow-dir changes. Wanting an upstream workflow = deliberate local commit with a user token | `(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED)` |

input.prompt:
Adversarial code review of commit d1103041b in C:\Users\khair\Code Projects\fork-superset-windows-arm64 (run `git show d1103041b`). Context: the file apps/desktop/src/main/lib/agent-jsonl-watcher/pane-map-hook.ts embeds a Python hook script (superset-notify.py) inside a TS template literal `const NOTIFY_SCRIPT = \`...\``. Hard constraints on the embedded Python: NO backticks anywhere inside the template, NO regex/single-backslash escapes (they get swallowed by the template literal — string ops only; `\\n` in TS source is intentional and becomes `\n` in the emitted python), must never raise (dot decisions must fail toward "keep hold" = yellow, never toward false-green), and JSON cache file `<terminalId>.teamstate.json` schema bumped v2→v3.

The change: teammate-idle drop decision moved from a global "all tracked names idle" predicate to per-entry matching — a background_tasks[] teammate entry's `description` (spawn prompt head, first ~50 chars + "..."), whitespace-normalized, is prefix-matched against prompt prefixes recorded at each named Agent spawn (`prompts[name] = _team_norm(prompt)[:160]`); drop only when ≥1 match AND all matching names' ledger state == "idle". Plus: SendMessage to a known fork name (fork_tools.values()) no longer marks active; teammate-message ids containing " [" mirror their state onto the base name.

Verify specifically:
1. Failure direction: every new code path on exception/None/odd shape keeps entries (yellow), never drops.
2. Prefix-match soundness: normalized desc prefix vs stored [:160] prompt — any case where a RUNNING teammate's entry could be dropped falsely (e.g. two spawns, same prompt, one idle one active — does candidate set include both? base-name mirror interactions with respawned duplicate names).
3. The truncation edge: description shorter than prompt with no "..." suffix; description with trailing whitespace before "..."; _team_norm applied consistently on both sides.
4. Cache v3: v2 file rejected → full rescan; offset>size reset also resets prompts; partial-write torn JSON handled.
5. Template safety: no backticks, no `${`, no single-backslash regex in the changed region; b"\\n" usage preserved.
6. Callers: _without_idle_teammates signature/behavior change vs its call site (Stop/SubagentStop only), note logging (only when dropped>0 — is that a regression vs before? previously note appended whenever drop happened; fine), finished teammate entries now pass through instead of being dropped — confirm downstream consumers (_split_background, _running_bg_ids, agentbg/bgactive snapshot writers) skip finished entries so behavior is unchanged.
7. FEATURES.md markers block line "(TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH)\tapps/desktop/src/main" — token must literally appear in a file under apps/desktop/src/main (it does, in comments — confirm exact spelling parity).

Return: verdict (ship / fix-first) + numbered findings with severity and file:line. Be skeptical; try to construct concrete false-green scenarios.

content.text:
Review agent running in background — will proceed to build trigger when its verdict lands.

**Status so far:**

**Root cause (confirmed with live evidence):** Yellow dot on the PX `popular-throat` chat was held by `background_tasks[]` reporting 25 finished "Opus wrangler" teammates as `status:"running"` forever (known Claude Code harness bug). The (TEAMMATE-IDLE) ledger fix that should drop them never fired: its all-idle predicate required *every* tracked name idle, and the ledger had 55 permanently-"active" names that can never idle — named non-fork agents (sol/general-purpose/`*-review-fable`) that finish via task-notification not idle_notification, forks re-marked active by SendMessage, and `name [hash]` duplicate-suffix splits. Poisoned predicate → drop never fires → permanent yellow. Every heavy multi-agent session reproduces this.

**Fix shipped to main (d1103041b, TEAM-ENTRY-MATCH):** drop decision now per-entry — entry description (spawn-prompt head) matched against prompt prefixes recorded at spawn; drop only when matched and all matching names provably idle. Plus SendMessage-fork poison fix + suffix mirror. Verified against the actual poisoned session: all 9 captured stuck descriptions matched + dropped; active/unmatched/short/shell/finished all kept. esbuild + biome clean, staged blobs 0 CR.

**Next:** Fable review verdict → trigger full gh build → AskUser for install timing.
